findings.reachability: Any unauthenticated visitor on the internet; registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_ (and wc_ajax_ which is also public/unauthenticated). The phpcs:ignore comment at line 35 explicitly suppresses the nonce-check warning, confirming intentional omission.
findings.justification: getResultDetails() reads $_POST['items'] with no nonce or referer verification whatsoever; a cross-origin request can be forged to make any logged-in user's browser query product/taxonomy detail HTML on their behalf, leaking whatever canUserSee() would permit for that user's session — a read-only CSRF whose value is limited but not zero (e.g., probing whether a private product exists for an admin user).

detection_pattern.section_name: CSRF: public AJAX handler without nonce
detection_pattern.description: Finds wp_ajax_nopriv_ registrations (publicly callable AJAX handlers) and flags those whose corresponding callback does not contain a nonce verification call. Even read-only handlers can leak data via CSRF if the response differs by auth context.

findings.reachability: Requires a third-party plugin or theme that hooks the filter dgwt/wcas/suggestion_details/taxonomy/headline; not reachable by an unauthenticated attacker in a default installation.
findings.justification: $title is assembled with safe HTML strings (esc_html applied to $termName at line 211) and then passed through apply_filters() at line 213-219, after which the full $title string is echo'd without any escaping at line 223. Any plugin hooking dgwt/wcas/suggestion_details/taxonomy/headline and returning malicious HTML would have it injected verbatim into the response; the risk is low because filter hooks require server-level access, but the pattern breaks the contract that caller-owned HTML should be escaped at the final output point.

detection_pattern.description: Catches direct echo of apply_filters() return values without an escaping wrapper. Filter hooks can be registered by any installed plugin, so unescaped filter output is a latent stored-XSS vector whenever the filter return value is not already trusted HTML.

findings.reachability: Any caller of the public AJAX endpoint.
findings.justification: The handler uses echo json_encode(...) instead of wp_send_json() or wp_send_json_success(); WordPress's wp_send_json() calls wp_die() with the correct application/json Content-Type, while the raw echo leaves the response with the default text/html Content-Type. Browsers may MIME-sniff the JSON response as HTML in legacy modes, potentially executing embedded script fragments injected via the dgwt/wcas/suggestion_details/output filter.

detection_pattern.section_name: Missing Content-Type: JSON response sent as text/html
detection_pattern.description: Finds AJAX handlers that build a JSON response with echo json_encode() instead of wp_send_json(). Without an explicit Content-Type: application/json header, browsers may MIME-sniff the response and execute embedded HTML/script content.
